scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines explicitly attributes this law to 'the same lawgiver' (Solon) who established the foundations of the Athenian legal code.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines is prosecuting Timarchus under the existing laws of the Athenian democracy concerning the qualifications of public speakers.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech 'Against Timarchus' was delivered in 345 BCE, representing the contemporary legal standards of the period.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule regarding disenfranchisement (atimia) for male prostitution was a core component of the Athenian legal code governing citizen behavior.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The law specifically protected Athenian citizens from being hired for prostitution as a form of hubris against the citizen body.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The protection of orphans was a standard administrative duty of the Athenian Archon Eponymus during the Classical period.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines references this as an established contemporary duty of the Athenian archons in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion of those who had lost their reputation through prostitution from the agora was a specific Athenian civic penalty.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines describes this restriction as an active part of the Athenian penal system in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim explicitly identifies the 'Council of Elders' (Gerousia) and the 'Lacedaemonians' (Spartans).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines attributes the laws governing the exclusion and punishment of adulteresses specifically to the lawgiver Solon.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines cites this capital punishment for procurers as part of the ancestral Solonian legislation.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion from priesthoods due to bodily 'impurity' (prostitution) was a specific religious and civic regulation in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines cites this as a contemporary disqualification for Athenian public and religious office in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines explicitly refers to this as a law enacted by Solon to maintain order in the assembly.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The proclamation prioritizing speakers over fifty was a specific procedural rule of the Athenian Ecclesia.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines laments the decline of this practice in his speech 'Against Ctesiphon', delivered in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The mandatory audit (euthyna) for all public officials was a hallmark of the Athenian democratic system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule is central to the legal arguments in the speech 'Against Ctesiphon' in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The inclusion of priests and priestesses in the mandatory audit process was a feature of Athenian law's oversight of public trusts.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines cites this as a contemporary law during the trial in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Trierarchs, despite funding the state, were subject to the standard Athenian audit (euthyna).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines discusses this as a binding legal requirement in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The prohibition of crowning officials before their audit was the specific legal crux of the dispute between Aeschines and Demosthenes.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech 'Against Ctesiphon' focuses on this specific law as it stood in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Travel restrictions on officials pending audit were part of Athens' strict accountability measures.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines cites this as an existing statute at the time of the trial in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Property restrictions (the inability to dispose of or dedicate assets) pending audit were standard in the Athenian euthyna process.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This is presented as a contemporary legal prohibition in Athens in 330 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athenian law strictly regulated the location where public honors (crowns) could be announced to preserve democratic decorum.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines references this regulation as a key contemporary law in 330 BCE.
